Q1. Generative vs Discriminative Models¶

Feature Discriminative Generative
Goal Learn boundary between classes Learn distribution of data
Learns P(Y|X) — probability of label given input P(X,Y) or P(X) — joint/data distribution
Output Class label / decision New data (text, image, audio)
Training data Needs labeled data Can be unsupervised or self-supervised
Examples Logistic Regression, SVM, BERT (classifier), Random Forest GPT, DALL-E, VAE, GAN, Stable Diffusion
Use case Spam detection, sentiment, classification Text generation, image synthesis, translation

Simple analogy¶

Discriminative:  Given a photo → is it a cat or dog? (draws a boundary)
Generative:      Given nothing → generate a photo of a cat (learns what a cat looks like)

How Generative Models work (self-supervised)¶

GPT:   Input: "The cat sat on the ___"  → predict next token → learns language distribution
BERT:  Input: "The [MASK] sat on mat"  → predict masked token → learns context (discriminative backbone)
GAN:   Generator creates fake images → Discriminator tries to detect fake → both improve together

↑ Back to Index

Q2. Tokens & Embeddings — How They Work Together¶

Tokens¶

  • A token is the smallest unit of text a model processes
  • Not always a word — can be subword, character, or word piece
  • Tokenizer converts raw text → sequence of integer IDs
Text:    "unhappiness"
Tokens:  ["un", "happiness"]        ← subword tokenization (BPE)
IDs:     [1045, 8398]

Text:    "Hello world!"
Tokens:  ["Hello", " world", "!"]   ← ~3 tokens
Rule of thumb: 1 token ≈ 0.75 words ≈ 4 characters (English)

Tokenization algorithms¶

Algorithm Used by How Package
BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) GPT-2, GPT-4, Llama Merge most frequent pairs iteratively from tokenizers import Tokenizer
WordPiece BERT Similar to BPE but maximizes language model likelihood from transformers import AutoTokenizer
SentencePiece T5, Gemini Language-agnostic subword, works on raw text import sentencepiece as spm
Tiktoken GPT-3.5/4 (OpenAI) Faster BPE implementation import tiktoken

Embeddings¶

  • An embedding is a dense vector of floats representing a token (or sentence) in high-dimensional space
  • Similar meanings → vectors close together (small cosine distance)
  • Token IDs → looked up in an embedding matrix → vector of size d_model (e.g. 768, 1536, 4096)
Embedding matrix shape: [vocab_size × d_model]
e.g. GPT-2: [50257 × 768]

token_id = 1045  →  lookup row 1045  →  [0.23, -0.11, 0.87, ..., 0.04]  (768 floats)

How they work together¶

Raw text
   ↓  Tokenizer
Token IDs:   [9906, 1917, 0]          ("Hello", " world", "!")
   ↓  Embedding lookup
Vectors:     [[0.2, -0.1, ...],       (each token → 768-dim vector)
              [0.5,  0.3, ...],
              [-0.1, 0.9, ...]]
   ↓  + Positional Encoding
Input to Transformer

Why embeddings capture meaning¶

king  - man + woman ≈ queen        (famous word2vec analogy)
Paris - France + Italy ≈ Rome      (semantic arithmetic works in vector space)

↑ Back to Index

Q6. Types of Chunking¶

Chunking = splitting documents into pieces before embedding. The most important RAG decision.

Type How Advantage Disadvantage When to use
Fixed size Split every N characters/tokens (500 tokens+overlap) Simple, fast, predictable Can split mid-sentence, breaks context Baseline; uniform text
Recursive Sentence Split at sentence boundaries Preserves sentence meaning Sentences vary in size; short = low context FAQ, short factual docs
Recursive Paragraph Split at paragraph/double newline Natural topic boundary Paragraphs vary widely in size Articles, reports
Recursive Try separators in order: \n\n → \n → . → → char Respects structure, flexible More complex General purpose (LangChain default)
Semantic (with meaning) Split where embedding similarity drops (topic shift) Semantically coherent chunks Slow; needs embedding every sentence,expensive Long mixed-topic docs
Document structure Split by headings, sections (HTML tags, Markdown headers) Preserves document structure Format-specific PDFs, Markdown, HTML docs
Agentic / Propositions LLM rewrites doc into atomic factual statements Very high retrieval precision Expensive (LLM call per doc) High-accuracy RAG systems
contextial retreval context+ chunk better Format-specific cosyly

heirarchical

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=500,
    chunk_overlap=50
)

chunks = splitter.split_text(text)



from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker

splitter = SemanticChunker(embeddings)

Best for:

  • RAG
  • Knowledge bases
  • Long documents

Chunk size trade-off¶

Small chunks (128–256 tokens):
  + Precise retrieval (matches narrow query well)
  - Missing context (answer may span multiple chunks)

Large chunks (512–1024 tokens):
  + More context per chunk
  - Noisy retrieval (relevant part buried in chunk)
  - Fewer chunks fit in LLM context window

Overlap (e.g. 20% overlap between chunks):
  → Prevents answer from falling at chunk boundary
  → Common: chunk_size=512, overlap=50

How to choose¶

Short Q&A / FAQs           → Sentence or small fixed (256 tokens)
Technical docs / manuals   → Recursive or document structure
Long research papers       → Semantic or paragraph with overlap
Legal / contracts          → Document structure (sections/clauses)
High accuracy required     → Agentic / proposition chunking

↑ Back to Index

Q7. Types of Embeddings¶

Type What it embeds Example models Use case
Word embeddings Single words Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText Classic NLP, word similarity
Sentence embeddings Full sentence/passage SBERT, E5, BGE, GTE Semantic search, RAG, clustering
Document embeddings Long documents Longformer, Doc2Vec Document similarity, classification
Cross-encoder Pair (query, doc) → relevance score BERT fine-tuned Re-ranking retrieved results
Multi-modal embeddings Text + image together CLIP, ImageBind Image-text search
Code embeddings Source code CodeBERT, StarCoder embeddings Code search, similarity
Sparse embeddings TF-IDF style weighted word vectors SPLADE, BM25 Lexical keyword search
Dense embeddings Continuous vector, meaning-based text-embedding-3, E5-large Semantic similarity

Dense vs Sparse¶

Dense:   [0.23, -0.11, 0.87, 0.04, ...]   1536 non-zero floats
         Captures semantic meaning (synonyms match)
         "car" ≈ "automobile"  → close in dense space

Sparse:  [0, 0, 0, 3.2, 0, 0, 1.1, 0, ...]  mostly zeros
         Captures exact keyword match (like BM25/TF-IDF)
         "car" ≠ "automobile"  unless both in query

Hybrid:  Dense + Sparse combined → best of both (Weaviate, Pinecone hybrid search)

Bi-encoder vs Cross-encoder¶

Bi-encoder (e.g. SBERT):
  Embed query and doc independently → compare with cosine similarity
  Fast — pre-compute doc embeddings → O(1) lookup
  Use for: retrieval (first stage)

Cross-encoder (e.g. fine-tuned BERT):
  Takes [query + doc] together → outputs relevance score 0-1
  Accurate — attends across both — but slow (can't pre-compute)
  Use for: re-ranking top-K retrieved results (second stage)

RAG pipeline:  Bi-encoder retrieval → Cross-encoder re-ranking → LLM

Popular embedding models (2024-2025)¶

| Model | Dim | Best for | Best for | |-------|-----|----------|----------| | text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI) | 1536 | General, cheap, fast |from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer| | text-embedding-3-large (OpenAI) | 3072 | Best OpenAI accuracy |from openai import OpenAI| | E5-large-v2 (Microsoft) | 1024 | Open source, strong | | BGE-M3 (BAAI) | 1024 | Multi-lingual, hybrid | | GTE-large | 1024 | Strong general purpose | | SBERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) | 384 | Fast, lightweight, local |

↑ Back to Index

In [ ]:

Q3. Transformer Architecture — Attention Is All You Need¶

Why Transformers are powerful¶

1. Parallelism     → process all tokens at once (not sequential like RNN)
2. Long range      → any token can attend to any other in O(1) steps
3. Scalable        → more layers + more heads + bigger FFN = better
4. Transfer learn  → pre-train once on huge data, fine-tune for anything
5. Attention maps  → interpretable — can see what the model focuses on

Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder¶

Architecture Models Use case
Encoder only BERT, RoBERTa Classification, embeddings, understanding
Decoder only GPT, Llama, Claude Text generation, completion
Encoder-Decoder T5, BART, mT5 Translation, summarization, Q&A
Feature GPT BERT
Full Form Generative Pre-trained Transformer Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers
Architecture Decoder-only Transformer Encoder-only Transformer
Main Purpose Generate text Understand text
Attention Causal (left-to-right) Bidirectional
Training Objective Predict the next token Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
Best For Chatbots, content generation, coding, summarization Search, classification, semantic similarity, embeddings
Output Generates new text Produces contextual representations/embeddings
Used in RAG Generator (LLM that produces the final answer) Retriever, reranker, or embedding model
Context Visibility Can only see previous tokens Can see both previous and future tokens
Example Models GPT-3, GPT-4, GPT-4o BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT, SBERT
Primary Use Cases Text generation, code generation, Q&A, translation Sentiment analysis, NER, search, document retrieval
Inference Style Autoregressive (token-by-token generation) Encodes the entire input simultaneously
Strength Fluent text generation and reasoning Deep language understanding and semantic representation
Limitation Not optimized purely for retrieval/classification Cannot naturally generate long-form text

Interview Summary¶

Self-Attention

  • Q, K, and V come from the same sequence.
  • Learns relationships within a sentence.
  • Used in GPT and BERT.

Cross-Attention

  • Q comes from one sequence, K and V come from another.
  • Connects information between two sequences.
  • Used in encoder-decoder models like T5 and BART.

Easy way to remember¶

  • Self-Attention: "I am looking at myself."
  • Cross-Attention: "I am looking at another sequence."

Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention¶

Feature Self-Attention Cross-Attention
Definition Tokens attend to other tokens within the same sequence Tokens attend to tokens from another sequence
Query (Q) Comes from the current sequence Comes from the decoder/current sequence
Key (K) Comes from the same sequence Comes from the encoder/another sequence
Value (V) Comes from the same sequence Comes from the encoder/another sequence
Used In Transformer Encoder, GPT Decoder, BERT Encoder-Decoder models like T5, BART, original Transformer
Purpose Learn relationships within a sentence Connect information between two different sequences
Example Understanding context within a sentence Translating English to French or attending to retrieved documents

↑ Back to Index

In [ ]:

Q4. Top-K, Top-P & Temperature¶

These control how the model samples the next token from the probability distribution.

Temperature --- logit->scale the logit by temp values - soft max with /T¶

Scales the logits before softmax:
  P(token) = softmax(logits / temperature)

Temperature = 1.0  → original distribution (default)
Temperature < 1.0  → sharper distribution → more confident → more deterministic / repetitive
Temperature > 1.0  → flatter distribution → more uniform → more random / creative
Temperature → 0    → always picks highest probability token (greedy decoding)

Top-K¶

Keep only the K highest probability tokens, set rest to 0, renormalize.

Top-K = 3:
  Full vocab: [cat:0.5, dog:0.3, car:0.1, tree:0.05, ...]
  After Top-3: [cat:0.5, dog:0.3, car:0.1] → renormalize → sample from these 3 only

Problem: K=50 might include irrelevant tokens when dist is sharp,
         or exclude good tokens when dist is flat.

Top-P (Nucleus Sampling)¶

Keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability ≥ P.

Top-P = 0.9:
  Sort by prob:  cat:0.5, dog:0.3, car:0.1, tree:0.06, ...
  Cumulative:    0.5 → 0.8 → 0.9  ← stop here
  Nucleus:       [cat, dog, car] → renormalize → sample

Advantage: dynamic — adapts to sharp (few tokens) or flat (many tokens) distributions

Comparison¶

Parameter Controls Range Low value High value
Temperature Sharpness of distribution 0 → ∞ Deterministic, repetitive Random, creative
Top-K Fixed number of candidates 1 → vocab_size Focused (top 1 = greedy) More variety
Top-P Dynamic probability mass 0 → 1 Fewer candidates More candidates

Typical settings¶

Factual / coding tasks:    temperature=0.2, top_p=0.9   (focused, accurate)
General chat:              temperature=0.7, top_p=0.95  (balanced)
Creative writing:          temperature=1.2, top_p=0.95  (diverse, surprising)
Deterministic/debug:       temperature=0,  top_k=1      (greedy — same output every time)

↑ Back to Index

Q5. RAG Architecture (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)¶

Problem RAG solves¶

LLMs have a knowledge cutoff and can hallucinate.
RAG = give the LLM relevant external documents at query time → grounded answers.

Full RAG Architecture¶

INDEXING PIPELINE (offline — done once)
────────────────────────────────────────
Documents (PDF, web, DB)
   ↓  Chunking
Text chunks (e.g. 512 tokens each)
   ↓  Embedding model (e.g. text-embedding-3-small)
Dense vectors  [0.23, -0.11, ..., 0.87]  (1536-dim)
   ↓  Store
Vector Database (Pinecone, Weaviate, ChromaDB, FAISS)


QUERY PIPELINE (online — per user question)
────────────────────────────────────────────
User query: "What is our refund policy?"
   ↓  Same embedding model
Query vector
   ↓  ANN search in vector DB (top-K similar chunks)
Retrieved chunks (e.g. top 5 most similar)
   ↓  Prompt construction
Prompt: "Context: [chunk1][chunk2]...  Question: What is our refund policy?"
   ↓  LLM (GPT-4, Claude, Llama)
Answer grounded in retrieved documents

RAG variants¶

Type Description When to use
Naive RAG Basic retrieve → generate Simple Q&A
Advanced RAG Re-ranking, query rewriting, HyDE Better accuracy needed
Modular RAG Plug-in components (router, fusion, summarizer) Complex pipelines
Agentic RAG LLM decides when/what to retrieve Multi-hop, tool-use
Graph RAG Knowledge graph + vector retrieval Relational/structured knowledge

Key components to optimize¶

1. Chunking strategy    → chunk size + overlap affects retrieval quality
2. Embedding model      → must match domain; same model for index + query
3. Retrieval (top-K)    → too few = miss info; too many = noise in context
4. Re-ranking           → cross-encoder scores retrieved chunks for relevance
5. Context window       → fit retrieved chunks + query into LLM context limit
6. Prompt template      → how context is presented to LLM matters

rag types:¶

Standard RAG – Retrieve once and generate.
Hybrid RAG – Combine dense vector search with keyword search.
Corrective RAG (CRAG) – Validate and improve retrieved results before answering.
Self-RAG – The model decides whether it needs more retrieval and critiques its own output.
Agentic RAG / ReAct – The model plans, retrieves, reasons, and may use tools in multiple steps.
RAPTOR / Hierarchical RAG – Organize documents into hierarchical summaries for scalable retrieval.
Fusion and Multi-Source RAG – Aggregate information from multiple retrievers or data sources for better coverage.

↑ Back to Index

Q8. Vector DB Architecture — How Data is Stored¶

What is a Vector DB?¶

A database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional vectors using Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search.

How data is stored¶

Each record = { id, vector, metadata }

id:        "doc_001"
vector:    [0.23, -0.11, 0.87, ..., 0.04]   (1536 floats = ~6KB)
metadata:  {"source": "manual.pdf", "page": 5, "chunk": "Returns policy..."}

Storage layers:
  1. Vector store    → flat files or segments (like SSTable) with float arrays
  2. Index structure → HNSW graph or IVF clusters for fast search
  3. Metadata store  → separate KV store or column store for filtering
  4. WAL / log       → durability (write-ahead log)

Core operations¶

INSERT:  embed text → store (id, vector, metadata)
SEARCH:  embed query → find top-K nearest vectors (cosine/dot/L2)
FILTER:  metadata filter first → then ANN search on subset (pre-filter or post-filter)
DELETE:  mark vector as deleted; compaction removes it
UPDATE:  delete + re-insert (vectors are immutable)

Distance metrics¶

Metric Formula (simplified) Best for
Cosine similarity angle between vectors Text (direction matters, not magnitude)
Dot product magnitude × cosine Normalized vectors (same as cosine if unit vectors)
Euclidean (L2) straight-line distance Image embeddings, absolute position matters

Popular Vector DBs¶

DB Index Hosted Notes
Pinecone HNSW + IVF ✅ Managed Production-ready, easy API
Weaviate HNSW Both Hybrid search, GraphQL
Qdrant HNSW Both Fast, Rust, filtering
ChromaDB HNSW Self-hosted Local dev, LangChain default
FAISS IVF, HNSW, PQ Library Meta, high performance, no server
pgvector IVFFlat, HNSW PostgreSQL ext SQL + vectors together
Milvus IVF, HNSW Both Large scale, enterprise

↑ Back to Index

Q9. Vector DB Index Types¶

Why indexing?¶

Brute-force search: compare query to every vector → O(n × d) → too slow at scale
ANN index:          approximate search → O(log n) or O(√n) → 99% accuracy, 1000× faster

Index Types¶

1. Flat (Brute Force)¶

No index — compute exact similarity to every vector.
Accuracy: 100% (exact)
Speed:    Slow (O(n))
Use when: < 10K vectors, accuracy critical, testing/debug

2. IVF — Inverted File Index¶

Step 1: Cluster vectors into K centroids (K-means)
Step 2: At query time, find nearest centroid(s)
Step 3: Search only vectors in those cluster(s)

nlist   = number of clusters (e.g. 100)
nprobe  = how many clusters to search (higher = more accurate, slower)

Accuracy: Good (tunable with nprobe)
Speed:    Fast (only search subset)
Use when: 100K – 10M vectors, batch indexing OK

3. HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World (like skip list)¶

Builds a multi-layer graph:
  Layer 2 (top):   few nodes, long-range connections   → coarse navigation
  Layer 1 (mid):   more nodes, medium connections
  Layer 0 (base):  all nodes, short-range connections  → fine search

Query: start at top layer → greedy walk toward query → descend layers → find neighbors

M    = max connections per node (higher = better accuracy, more memory)
efC  = construction beam width (higher = better index, slower build)
efS  = search beam width (higher = better recall, slower search)

Accuracy: Very high (>99% recall)
Speed:    Very fast (log n)
Memory:   High (stores the graph)
Use when: < 10M vectors, low-latency needed (default in Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant)

4. PQ — Product Quantization¶

Compress vectors: split 1536-dim vector into M subvectors → quantize each to 256 codes
1536 floats × 4 bytes = 6KB per vector  →  M bytes per vector (8-32× compression)

Accuracy: Lower (lossy compression)
Memory:   Very low
Use when: Billions of vectors, memory constrained
Often combined: IVF+PQ (FAISS default for large scale)

5. LSH — Locality Sensitive Hashing¶

Hash similar vectors into same bucket using random projections.
Query = hash → lookup bucket → compare within bucket only.

Accuracy: Lower
Speed:    Very fast
Use when: Very high throughput, some accuracy loss acceptable

Summary¶

Index Accuracy Speed Memory Scale
Flat 100% Slowest Low <10K
IVF High Fast Medium 100K–10M
HNSW Very high Very fast High <10M
IVF+PQ Medium Fast Very low 10M–1B
LSH Medium Fastest Low High throughput

Annoy ann oh yeah from spotify (randomforest)

↑ Back to Index

Q10. Fine-tuning, PEFT, BERT, LoRA, QLoRA, Adapters¶

Full Fine-tuning¶

Take a pre-trained LLM → update ALL parameters on your task-specific data.

Problem:
  GPT-3:   175B parameters × 4 bytes = 700 GB  → needs multiple A100 GPUs
  LLaMA-2: 70B parameters             → needs ~140 GB GPU memory
  Expensive, slow, risk of catastrophic forgetting.

PEFT — Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning¶

Why introduced:
  Full fine-tune is too expensive for most use cases.
  PEFT: freeze most of the model → only train a small number of new/modified parameters.
  Result: similar performance to full fine-tune at 0.1–1% of the compute cost.

Full fine-tune: update 100% of parameters
PEFT:           update 0.1–5% of parameters

BERT¶

BERT = Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (Google, 2018)
Architecture: Encoder-only Transformer
Training:     Masked Language Modeling (MLM) + Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
Reads text:   bidirectionally (sees left AND right context simultaneously)

Use cases: Classification, NER, QA, embeddings — NOT text generation
Fine-tuning: Add task-specific head on top of [CLS] token → train whole model

BERT-base:  12 layers, 110M params
BERT-large: 24 layers, 340M params

LoRA — Low Rank Adaptation¶

Key insight: weight updates during fine-tuning have low intrinsic rank.
             → approximate ΔW with two small matrices instead of full matrix.

Original weight matrix W: shape [d × k]  (e.g. 4096 × 4096 = 16M params)
LoRA: freeze W, add   W + ΔW = W + B × A
      A: shape [r × k]  (r << d,  e.g. r=8)
      B: shape [d × r]
      Trainable params = d×r + r×k  (e.g. 4096×8 + 8×4096 = 65K  vs  16M)

LoRA rank r:
  r=4–8:   lightweight, good for style/tone changes
  r=16–32: better for domain adaptation
  r=64+:   approaches full fine-tune quality

WHERE in LLM:
  Applied to Transformer attention weight matrices:
  Q (query), K (key), V (value), and output projection Wₒ
  Sometimes also FFN (feed-forward) layers
  Stage: during fine-tuning (after pre-training), not during pre-training

At inference: merge B×A back into W → zero extra latency

QLoRA — Quantized LoRA¶

LoRA + Quantization of the base model weights.

Base model: quantized to 4-bit (NF4 — NormalFloat4) → 4× memory reduction
LoRA adapters: kept in 16-bit (bfloat16) → still trainable

Result:
  LLaMA-2-70B full fine-tune: ~140 GB GPU
  QLoRA on same model:        ~48 GB GPU  (fits on 2× A100 40GB)

Key techniques in QLoRA:
  1. NF4 quantization     → optimal for normally distributed weights
  2. Double quantization  → quantize the quantization constants too (saves ~0.4 bits/param)
  3. Paged optimizers     → offload optimizer states to CPU RAM to prevent OOM

Adapters¶

Insert small trainable modules (bottleneck layers) between Transformer layers.
Freeze original model → only adapter layers trained.

Structure of one adapter:
  Input (d) → Linear down (d → m) → GELU → Linear up (m → d) → + residual → Output (d)
  m << d  (bottleneck, e.g. d=768, m=64)

Difference from LoRA:
  Adapters:  add new layers between existing layers → small inference overhead
  LoRA:      modifies existing weight matrices directly → zero inference overhead (after merge)

LoRA is generally preferred today (faster inference, equally efficient training).

Summary Table¶

Method Params trained Memory Inference overhead Best for
Full fine-tune 100% Very high None Best accuracy, have compute
Adapter ~1–3% (new layers) Low Small (extra layers) Task-specific add-ons
LoRA ~0.1–1% (low-rank) Low None (merged) General PEFT, most popular
QLoRA ~0.1–1% (4-bit base) Very low None (merged) Consumer GPU, large models
Prompt tuning Soft prompt tokens only Minimal Tiny Simple task adaptation

Where LoRA fits in the LLM lifecycle¶

Stage 1: Pre-training       → Train on trillion tokens from internet (full training)
                               Result: base model (e.g. LLaMA-3-base)

Stage 2: SFT with LoRA      → Fine-tune on instruction/task data (LoRA applied here)
                               Result: instruction-following model

Stage 3: RLHF / DPO + LoRA → Align with human preferences (LoRA applied here too)
                               Result: chat model (e.g. LLaMA-3-instruct)

Stage 4: Domain fine-tune   → Company-specific data (LoRA / QLoRA)
                               Result: domain-specific assistant

LoRA is used at Stages 2, 3, 4 — never during pre-training (Stage 1).

↑ Back to Index

LoRA vs QLoRA¶

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) are Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques that allow you to fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs) without updating all model parameters.

The main difference is that QLoRA loads the base model in quantized form (4-bit), significantly reducing GPU memory usage.

Comparison Table¶

Feature LoRA QLoRA
Full Form Low-Rank Adaptation Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation
Base Model FP16/BF16 4-bit Quantized
Train Base Model? ❌ Frozen ❌ Frozen
Trainable Parameters Only LoRA adapters Only LoRA adapters
GPU Memory Higher Much Lower
Quantization No Yes (4-bit NF4)
Training Speed Fast Slightly slower due to dequantization
Memory Efficiency Good Excellent
Accuracy High Nearly identical to LoRA in many tasks
Best For Medium-sized models Very large models on limited GPUs 
ΔW = A × B

where:

  • A is a low-rank matrix
  • B is a low-rank matrix

Only A and B are trained.

Final weight:

W' = W + A × B

Full Fine-Tuning
      │
      ▼
    LoRA
(Frozen Model + Adapters)
      │
      ▼
   QLoRA
(4-bit Frozen Model + Adapters)

from peft import LoraConfig

For QLoRA:

from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True
)

Q11. NVIDIA GPU Architectures (Ampere, Ada, Hopper, Blackwell)¶

NVIDIA GPU Architecture Timeline¶

Architecture Release Example GPUs Key AI Features Primary Use
Ampere 2020 A100, A30, RTX 3090 3rd Gen Tensor Cores, TF32, BF16, MIG AI training, HPC
Ada Lovelace 2022 RTX 4090, RTX 4080 4th Gen Tensor Cores, DLSS 3 Gaming, AI inference
Hopper 2022 H100, H200 Transformer Engine, FP8 LLM training & inference
Blackwell 2024 B100, B200, GB200 2nd Gen Transformer Engine, FP4/FP8, NVLink improvements Large-scale AI training and inference

What is CUDA?¶

CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture) is NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and programming model that enables developers to use NVIDIA GPUs for general-purpose computing, not just graphics rendering.

It allows thousands of threads to execute simultaneously, making GPUs ideal for:

  • Deep Learning
  • Machine Learning
  • Large Language Models (LLMs)
  • Scientific Computing
  • Computer Vision
  • High Performance Computing (HPC)

High-Level NVIDIA GPU Architecture¶

                    CPU
                     │
             PCIe / NVLink
                     │
                     ▼
          +----------------------+
          |      NVIDIA GPU      |
          +----------------------+
                     │
     ┌───────────────┼────────────────┐
     │               │                │
     ▼               ▼                ▼
+---------+     +---------+     +---------+
|   SM 1  |     |   SM 2  | ... |  SM N   |
+---------+     +---------+     +---------+
     │               │                │
     ▼               ▼                ▼
 CUDA Cores     Tensor Cores     RT Cores
     │               │
     └─────── Shared Memory ───────┘
                     │
                     ▼
                L2 Cache
                     │
                     ▼
          Global GPU Memory (HBM/GDDR)

NVIDIA GPU Architecture Components¶

Component Description Primary Function Example
Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) Main processing unit containing CUDA Cores, Tensor Cores, registers, and shared memory Executes thread blocks H100 contains many SMs running in parallel
CUDA Cores General-purpose parallel processing cores Perform arithmetic and logical operations Vector addition, matrix multiplication
Tensor Cores Specialized AI acceleration units Accelerate matrix multiplication for deep learning Transformer training, LLM inference
RT (Ray Tracing) Cores Dedicated hardware for ray tracing Accelerate lighting, reflections, and shadows Gaming and 3D rendering
Warp Group of 32 threads executed together Basic scheduling unit of GPU execution One warp = 32 parallel threads
Thread Block Collection of one or more warps Shares resources such as shared memory and executes on one SM 256 threads = 8 warps
Grid Collection of thread blocks launched by a CUDA kernel Organizes the complete GPU workload A kernel launching 100 blocks
Memory Hierarchy Registers → Shared Memory → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → Global Memory Provides different storage levels with varying speed and capacity Shared Memory for fast communication between threads

CUDA Execution Hierarchy¶

CUDA Kernel
      │
      ▼
    Grid
      │
      ▼
Thread Blocks
      │
      ▼
    Warps
 (32 Threads)
      │
      ▼
Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)
      │
 ┌────┴───────────────┐
 │                    │
 ▼                    ▼
CUDA Cores      Tensor Cores
      │
      ▼
Memory Hierarchy
(Registers → Shared → L1 → L2 → Global)

Memory Hierarchy¶

Memory Type Scope Speed Size Purpose
Registers Per Thread Fastest Small Store local variables
Shared Memory Per Block Very Fast Medium Share data among threads in a block
L1 Cache Per SM Fast Small Cache frequently accessed data
L2 Cache Entire GPU Moderate Larger Shared cache across SMs
Global Memory (HBM/GDDR) Entire GPU Slowest Largest Main storage for model weights and tensors

CUDA Programming Hierarchy¶

Kernel
   │
   ▼
Grid
   │
   ▼
Blocks
   │
   ▼
Warps (32 Threads)
   │
   ▼
Threads
   │
   ▼
CUDA Cores / Tensor Cores

Why Choose cuda:0, cuda:1, cuda:2 When Running an LLM? Pytorch¶

When a machine has multiple NVIDIA GPUs, each GPU is assigned an index:

Device Meaning
cuda:0 First GPU
cuda:1 Second GPU
cuda:2 Third GPU
cuda:3 Fourth GPU
... Additional GPUs

Bonus: GPU Architecture & CUDA¶

NVIDIA GPU Architecture Components¶

Component Description Primary Function
Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) Main processing unit containing CUDA Cores, Tensor Cores, registers, and shared memory Executes thread blocks
CUDA Cores General-purpose parallel processing cores Arithmetic and logical operations
Tensor Cores Specialized AI acceleration units Matrix multiplication for deep learning (transformer training, LLM inference)
Warp Group of 32 threads executed together Basic scheduling unit of GPU execution
Thread Block Collection of one or more warps Shares resources such as shared memory; executes on one SM
Grid Collection of thread blocks launched by a CUDA kernel Organizes the complete GPU workload

CUDA Execution Hierarchy¶

Kernel → Grid → Thread Blocks → Warps (32 Threads) → SM → CUDA / Tensor Cores
                                                          → Memory (Registers → Shared → L1 → L2 → Global)

Memory Hierarchy¶

Memory Type Scope Speed Purpose
Registers Per Thread Fastest Local variables
Shared Memory Per Block Very Fast Share data among threads in a block
L1 Cache Per SM Fast Cache frequently accessed data
L2 Cache Entire GPU Moderate Shared cache across SMs
Global Memory (HBM/GDDR) Entire GPU Slowest Main storage for model weights and tensors

Multi-GPU: cuda:0, cuda:1, cuda:2 (PyTorch)¶

# Each GPU gets an index
import torch
device = torch.device('cuda:0')   # First GPU
model = model.to('cuda:0')        # Load model onto GPU 0

# Distribute large model across GPUs
# LLaMA-70B won't fit in one GPU → split across cuda:0, cuda:1, cuda:2...
# Libraries: DeepSpeed, Accelerate, tensor_parallel

↑ Back to Index

The system should automatically query the Sales Data Mart, not HR or Finance.

High-Level Architecture¶

                        User
                          │
                          ▼
                   Chatbot Interface
                          │
                          ▼
                    Query Processor
                          │
         ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
         │                                 │
         ▼                                 ▼
   Intent Detection                Embedding Generation
         │                                 │
         └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
                        ▼
               Data Mart Router
                        │
      ┌─────────────────┼──────────────────┐
      │                 │                  │
      ▼                 ▼                  ▼
 Sales Mart       HR Mart          Finance Mart
      │                 │                  │
      ▼                 ▼                  ▼
 Vector Index     Vector Index      Vector Index
      │                 │                  │
      └──────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                     ▼
              Retrieve Top-K Chunks
                     │
                     ▼
                 Re-ranker
                     │
                     ▼
             Context Construction
                     │
                     ▼
                     LLM
                     │
                     ▼
              Natural Language Answer

                    User Query
                         │
                         ▼
                 Intent Classifier
                         │
             ┌───────────┴───────────┐
             │                       │
             ▼                       ▼
      Sales Mart              HR Mart
      Vector DB              Vector DB
             │                       │
             └───────────┬───────────┘
                         ▼
                Hybrid Retrieval
              (BM25 + Embeddings)
                         │
                         ▼
                  Cross-Encoder
                    Re-ranking
                         │
                         ▼
                 Merge Contexts
                         │
                         ▼
                        LLM
                         │
                         ▼
                    Final Answer


              "It's a Router-based Multi-Source Hybrid RAG architecture. An intent classifier routes the query to the relevant data mart(s), retrieval is performed using hybrid search (BM25 + vector embeddings), results are reranked with a cross-encoder, contexts from one or more marts are fused, and an LLM generates the final response while enforcing role-based access controls."

Bonus: Re-ranking Types & Packages¶

Re-ranking methods can be categorized based on how they score and reorder retrieved documents.

Type How It Works Accuracy Speed Common Models Best Use Case
Cross-Encoder Re-ranking Query and document are processed together to produce a relevance score ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Slow BGE Reranker, MonoBERT, Cohere Rerank Most production RAG systems
Bi-Encoder Re-ranking Compare query and document embeddings using cosine similarity ⭐⭐⭐ Fast SBERT, E5, BGE Large-scale retrieval
LLM-based Re-ranking An LLM evaluates retrieved documents and ranks them ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very Slow GPT, Claude, Llama High-quality reasoning tasks
Hybrid Re-ranking Combines lexical and semantic scores ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Medium BM25 + Embeddings Enterprise search
Rule-based Re-ranking Uses business rules to adjust ranking ⭐⭐ Very Fast Custom logic Priority documents
Learning-to-Rank (LTR) ML model trained on click or relevance data ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Medium LambdaMART, XGBoost Search engines
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) Combines rankings from multiple retrievers ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Fast RRF Hybrid retrieval
Metadata-based Re-ranking Prioritizes documents using metadata ⭐⭐ Very Fast Custom filters Date, source, user permissions
Contextual Re-ranking Considers user profile or conversation history ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Medium Custom models Personalized assistants

Popular Re-ranking Packages for RAG¶

Re-ranking packages help reorder retrieved documents so the most relevant ones are passed to the LLM.

Package Model Type Open Source Best For Example
sentence-transformers Cross-Encoder ✅ General RAG CrossEncoder("BAAI/bge-reranker-base")
FlagEmbedding BGE Reranker ✅ High-quality reranking FlagReranker
Cohere SDK Cohere Rerank API ❌ API Enterprise RAG co.rerank()
transformers Hugging Face models ✅ Custom rerankers AutoModelForSequenceClassification
ragatouille ColBERT ✅ Late interaction retrieval RAGPretrainedModel
PyTerrier Learning-to-Rank ✅ IR research pt.BatchRetrieve()
rank_bm25 Lexical ranking ✅ BM25 reranking BM25Okapi
Haystack Pipeline reranking ✅ End-to-end RAG TransformersSimilarityRanker
LlamaIndex Node reranking ✅ Document indexing SentenceTransformerRerank
LangChain Wrapper ✅ RAG pipelines CrossEncoderReranker

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

↑ Back to Index

Bonus: Hybrid Retrieval & RAG Architectures¶

Hybrid Retrieval combines keyword-based search (BM25) and semantic vector search (embeddings) to retrieve the most relevant documents.

The idea is to get the strengths of both approaches:

  • BM25 → Great for exact keyword matches.
  • Vector Search → Great for understanding meaning and synonyms.

Basic RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) Architecture¶

                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                           │      User Query     │
                           └──────────┬──────────┘
                                      │
                                      ▼
                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                           │  Query Embedding    │
                           │   (Embedding Model) │
                           └──────────┬──────────┘
                                      │
                                      ▼
                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                           │   Vector Database   │
                           │ (FAISS/Pinecone/    │
                           │  Qdrant/Milvus)     │
                           └──────────┬──────────┘
                                      │
                        Similarity Search (Cosine)
                                      │
                                      ▼
                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                           │ Retrieve Top-K Docs │
                           └──────────┬──────────┘
                                      │
                                      ▼
                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                           │ Context Builder     │
                           │ (Prompt Assembly)   │
                           └──────────┬──────────┘
                                      │
                    ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
                    │                                   │
                    ▼                                   ▼
              User Question                   Retrieved Context
                    │                                   │
                    └──────────────┬────────────────────┘
                                   ▼
                        ┌────────────────────────┐
                        │         LLM            │
                        │ (GPT / Llama / Claude) │
                        └──────────┬─────────────┘
                                   │
                                   ▼
                        ┌────────────────────────┐
                        │    Final Response      │
                        └────────────────────────┘

↑ Back to Index

Bonus: LangChain vs LangGraph vs LangSmith vs LlamaIndex¶

These frameworks serve different purposes in the LLM ecosystem. Although they are often used together, each solves a different problem.

Feature LangChain LangGraph LangSmith LlamaIndex
Primary Purpose Build LLM applications and pipelines Build stateful AI agents and workflows Monitor, debug, and evaluate LLM apps Data ingestion and RAG framework
Focus Chains and orchestration Agent workflows with state Observability and testing Retrieval and indexing
Best For Prompt pipelines, tools, RAG Multi-step agents, loops, decision making Tracing, debugging, evaluation Connecting LLMs to private data
State Management Limited ✅ Built-in Tracks execution Limited
Agent Support Basic Excellent Monitors agents Basic
RAG Support Good Uses LangChain components Evaluates RAG Excellent
Vector DB Integration ✅ Via LangChain N/A ✅ Native
Production Monitoring ❌ ❌ ✅ ❌
Typical Use Build chatbot Build autonomous agent Debug chatbot Build enterprise RAG

↑ Back to Index
